column2: Each cell = how ROW CHARACTER perceives COLUMN CHARACTER written in ROW CHARACTER's voice. Diagonal = X. Asymmetric by design. Each cell captures: current relationship state | emotional charge | any unresolved tension | arc direction (growing / decaying / static / unknown to them). WRITE IN PLAINTEXT ONLY. No markdown formatting of any kind (no asterisks for bold or italic no bullet points no headers no dashes as list items). Each cell is one continuous paragraph of plain prose in that character's voice.

Include all named minor characters in the graph — append (minor) to their name in the column and row headers. A minor character who never interacts with most of the cast is still load-bearing in their absence. Leave cells blank where there is genuinely no relationship or contact. Blank = no relationship. Do not fill blanks with filler. The pattern of blanks for a minor character (many blanks but one or two filled cells) is itself data about their structural role in the story.

Named societies and collective groups that function as characters in the world should also appear in the graph — append (society) to their name in column and row headers. Examples: a school and its student body (Harry Potter — Hogwarts); a criminal organization (the Death Eaters); a people defined by their shared oppression (the Winkies); a society that acts as a pressure system on individuals (Kansas prairie farming society). A society row and column will typically have many blank cells — it only interacts meaningfully with the characters whose lives it shapes. The filled cells carry the weight. Each cell from a society's row should be written in the collective voice of that group — what does this society perceive about the character? Each cell in a society's column should show how the individual character perceives and relates to that society as a force in their life. Societies do not have SELF cells — use a brief description of the society's self-image or collective identity instead.

Hogwarts School: perceives Harry as a famous student / burden / symbol. Ministry of Magic: perceives Harry as a threat to institutional stability. Death Eaters: perceive Harry as the one obstacle to their order. Gryffindor House: perceives Harry as their champion. These are all valid society rows and columns. Each has a different relationship to the same character and to each other.

Munchkinland society: perceives Dorothy as accidental liberator. Kansas prairie society: the force that shaped Dorothy and that she is trying to return to. Emerald City citizens: the audience for the Wizard's long performance. Winkies: the enslaved people whose liberation Dorothy caused without intending to. Winged Monkeys: a bound society whose freedom was a side effect of the story's resolution.

Add or remove columns and rows to match actual cast size. Column and row order must stay identical. A blank cell = these two characters have not met or are irrelevant to each other — intentional blanks are data. Minor characters often have many blank cells and one or two deeply loaded ones — that asymmetry is the point.
